key_insight: Computed materials proposals often fall outside the structural “memory” of experimentally discovered chemistry, and a historical structural map can rank how close each dataset stays to known basins.
engineering_relevance: A historical synthesizability prior can help materials engineers triage large candidate pools by favoring structures that are both structurally near known experimental communities and supported by reduced-formula precedent.
method: Embedded 167,500 ICSD entries in a continuous structural-similarity space.
Partitioned the space into graph communities and replayed discovery over time.
Projected computed datasets into frozen historical maps to compare their distance from experimental basins.
evidence_signal: Experimental discovery is highly path-dependent: 82.9% of new formulas entered pre-existing communities, and new-community formation dropped from 40.2% in the 1930s to 2.6% in the 2010s; held-out ICSD ranked above MatterGen, then GNoME≈Materials Project-theoretical, followed by JARVIS and Alexandria.
watch_item: Structural proximity is only one filter: the benchmark depends on ICSD coverage and community definitions, so synthesizability ranking should be treated as a triage signal rather than proof of realizability.
paper_title: Computed materials proposals depart from the structural memory of experimental discovery
